1610.title: Use canonical URL where possible in the "system" field of a ValueSet
1610.body:
The "system" field in the ValueSet is using OCL's relative URL, not the ValueSet's canonical URL (e.g. "system": "/orgs/CIEL/ValueSet/CIEL/") (see here for example: https://fhir.staging.openconceptlab.org/orgs/MER_SMART_Guidelines/ValueSet/art-medications/?version=v3) - In the example, I would expect CIEL's canonical URL "https://cielterminology.org/" to appear in the system field.

![image](https://github.com/OpenConceptLab/ocl_issues/assets/55999682/da32c3de-7e7f-4660-9ffd-8194f1a280f4)

nodes.body: In the long run, we probably want to use canonical URLs by default, but that's a decision we need to make.

2231.title: Dynamic reference cascade should account for equivalence to avoid false validation errors
2231.body:
When adding a concept to a source with cascade, we check for equivalence. This means, when considering the addition of any concept X during the cascade, if an existing concept in the source is mapped SAME-AS concept X, we do not add concept X and any mappings for it are adjusted to point the equivalent concept in the source as they are added.

For example, if the CIEL concept "Home electricity?" is being added and the source already has concepts mapped SAME-AS to its "Yes" and "No" answers, the concept is added with mappings to existing "Yes" and "No" concepts within the source.

When dynamic references are added to a collection, custom validation rules are run against the result of merging the cascaded results with the collection (without considering equivalency and removing duplicates), causing false duplicate name validation errors.

Using the previous example, when trying to add the CIEL concept "Home electricity?" a collection as a dynamic reference, the validation schema is run against the combination of the collection's existing concepts and the cascade results, which includes "Yes" and "No" in the collection (mapped SAME-AS to CIEL equivalents) _and_ the "Yes" and "No" from CIEL, resulting in duplicate name errors.

Two possible solutions:

1. **Add equivalency logic to custom validation**. In the context of adding a dynamic reference to a collection, adapt custom validation to replicate the equivalency logic, so any instances of the same source + code or cases where an existing concept has a SAME-AS mapping to a candidate concept, the candidate and any children would be ignored. This has the downside of needing to ensure the logic for equivalency checking for custom validation is kept aligned with the equivalency checking used when expanding a collection.

2. **Add equivalency checking to cascading of the dynamic references**. Adapt the logic of cascading dynamic references to account for equivalency, so any concepts that already exist in the collection (same source+code or mapped SAME-AS to the candidate concept) are skipped and any mappings to them are adjusted to point to the collection's existing concept. This has the advantage that validation would be run against the outcome of cascading instead of an interim (un-deduplicated) state, but may be more challenging or have performance costs.

1868.title: Enable creating/importing/editing resource in an older repo version
1868.body:
**Challenge:**
Version Model for resources and repo works on created_at. created_at is system generated datetime field, and cannot be tempered from outside. Whatever gets created later is the newer version.

**Tasks**
1. Need to establish a link between versions (linked list). Probably each versioned resource needs to have a URI of the previous
   - `previous_version_uri` -- will this affect standard checksum?
   - `version_order` -- for quick sorting
2. List versions -- Sort on all versions by `version_order` - 0 for initial version higher being newer
3. Latest version -- Sort on all versions by `version_order` and get max.
4. New version creation can have:
   - `new_version.previous_version_uri=existing_latest_version.uri` and `new_version.version_order=existing_latest_version.version_order + 1`
5. In between version creation needs to be created with `previous_version_uri` in payload. (considering the `new_version` is added after a version say `previous_version`. 
   - `existing_next_version` is version where `versions.previous_version_uri=pervious_version_uri`
   - `new_version.previous_version_uri=previous_version_uri` and `new_version.version_order=previous_version.version_order + 1`
   - `existing_next_version.previous_version_uri=new_version.uri` and `existing_next_version.version_order=new_version.version_order + 1`
6. `previous_version_uri` and `version_order` cannot be edited from outside.
7. Latest version will have `is_latest_version=true` just like now for resources. For Repo we must add this to make it consistent and faster to lookup.

@paynejd, we'll need to decide how you want to introduce these locale names into OCL (e.g., synonyms, new entries, or a combination).

It looks like Java locales are derived from [Unicode's Common Locale Data Repository (CLDR)](http://cldr.unicode.org/index). Their [download page](http://cldr.unicode.org/index/downloads) points to a GitHub repo with [CLDR json data](https://github.com/unicode-org/cldr-json#cldr-json). Java locales appear to be derived from [CLDR locale names](https://github.com/unicode-org/cldr-json/tree/master/cldr-json/cldr-localenames-modern/main).

A good summary of locales in Java is available in [Internationalization: Understanding Locale in the Java Platform](https://www.oracle.com/technical-resources/articles/javase/locale.html). From that article:

> If you want to know what `Locale` objects you can create, the answer is simple: You can create any locale you'd like.

In this case, perhaps we should just focus on supporting the locales that are needed right now – Indonesian (`in`) and British English (`en_GB`) and not try to support all possible Java locales.

Best Current Practice 47 for language tags ([BCP 47](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/bcp-index.txt)) is [RFC 5646](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5646), which uses hyphens to separate language and region codes. Because Java's `Locale` is ancient, it uses underscores instead of hyphens. Nice summary [here](https://softwareengineering.stackexchange.com/a/367706).

Per [Wikipedia's ISO 639-1 listing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-1_codes), the "in" representation for Indonesian language was replaced with "id" in 1989 ([ref](http://xml.coverpages.org/iso639a.html)). But this "in" designation still exists in Java (as listed above).

So, I've proposed adding "id" as a synonym for Indonesian language with type "ISO 639-1 Non-preferred". **NOTE: this is the first case of non-preferred for ISO 639-1. Previously, the only "non-preferred" language types were ISO 639-2 Non-preferred.**

I added an entry for British English as "en-GB" (following IETF BCP 47) and added synonyms "en-GB" and "en_GB". **NOTE: this introduces two new locale name types "IETF BCP 47" and "Java Locale".**

I created [oclapi2 PR-9](https://github.com/OpenConceptLab/oclapi2/pull/9) for these proposed changes.
@bmamlin @paynejd en_GB and ind are available on QA, Demo and Staging

Thanks @snyaggarwal.

FYI – for Indonesian, we need the legacy name "in" as name type "ISO 639-1 Non-preferred" as well. I manually added this to [staging](https://app.staging.openconceptlab.org/#/orgs/OCL/sources/Locales/concepts/ind/) before turning on the OpenMRS Custom Validation schema for the updated PIH dictionary.

1891.title: Freeze large resources upon deletion request
1891.body:
Deleting a large repo or org is pretty confusing for a user. There is no "Processing" flag. Content is not "frozen". They can continue to interact with the content while it is being processed asynchronously without any indication that it is being deleted.

Ideas for how we handle this:
- Implement "Frozen" flag -- content that is frozen can only be viewed; the flag should be visible to users
- When requesting a delete, OCL first "freezes" the content (assuming that this is a quick process?), and then kick off the deletion process

The idea is that "Freezing" content will be useful for other purposes: Loading a large repo; processing a large version; evaluating a large expansion; etc.

Finally, if I request a delete in TBv3, the UI should immediately respond, display a flag, etc.

nodes.body: Thanks @paynejd , this feels valuable for v3 for sure. Currently, I imagine that it's not super common to do large deletes right now, so I could imagine this development waiting for the v3 CodeSystem Author persona work. That said, if this is a low effort change, then I do think it's possible to put it into OCLv2 since the Frozen flag seems pretty simple from a UI perspective. I'll defer to you and @snyaggarwal though.

1867.title: Create independent script for standard and smart checksums for concepts and mappings
1867.body:
We want a script that can be run outside of the OCL core services. The script can be either python or javascript -- python might be easier for the moment. It must have the logic required to generate standard and smart checksums for concepts or mappings. The user should not need to know which fields should be included for standard/smart checksums -- this needs to be embedded in the script.

The initial goal is to enable @bmamlin to create checksums for CIEL resources outside of OCL to support validation workflows.

Also see #1691

nodes.body: Thanks @paynejd. A small python script that calculates checksum for a concept or a mapping would be very helpful. At this point, I'm [missing something](https://github.com/OpenConceptLab/ocl_issues/issues/1691#issuecomment-2081538642) in my little toy python script.

@snyaggarwal, if you could provide me with the structure of the JSON sent to the JSum algorithm for a CIEL concept (like [this one](https://api.openconceptlab.org/orgs/CIEL/sources/CIEL/concepts/1/)), it would help me troubleshoot my script.

Tasks:
- [ ] Create a python script in ocldev and reuse in API for checksum generation
- [ ] Create a JS script and convert it to a NPM package and publish
- [x] Both Python and JS script should generate close to identical output
- [x] Both scripts should accept args like resource type, checksum type and data

> @bmamlin For standard checksum --
> 
> ```
> {...}
> ```

Okay. That explains why I wasn't having any luck generating matching checksums from a CIEL import file. It's a bit more complicated than simply including or omitting properties by key. For example,
* `locale` is included on names & descriptions, but not for the concept itself
* `external_id` is included for the concept itself, but not for names & descriptions
* `child_concept_urls` and `parent_concept_urls` properties are included (not something I have outside of OCL)

```python
def get_mapping_fields(self, data):
    to_concept_code = getvalue(data, 'to_concept_code', None)
    to_concept_url = getvalue(data, 'to_concept_url', None)
    to_source_url = getvalue(data, 'to_source_url', None)

    from_source_version = getvalue(data, 'from_source_version', None)

    def expand_concept_url(concept_url, concept_code, source_url, source_version):
        if concept_url and (not concept_code or not source_url):
            url_parts = concept_url.split('/concepts/')  # /orgs/{org}/sources/{source}(/concepts/){concept}/
            if not concept_code:
                concept_code = url_parts[1].split('/')[0]
            if not source_url:

                if source_url.count('/') == 6:  # /orgs/{org}/sources/{source}/{source_version}/

                    source_url = '/'.join(source_url.split('/')[:-1])
        return concept_code, source_url, source_version
    to_concept_code, to_source_url, to_source_version = expand_concept_url(
        to_concept_url, to_concept_code, to_source_url, to_source_version)
    from_concept_code, from_source_url, from_source_version = expand_concept_url(

```

2057.title: Collection Importing via Version Export
2057.body:
While sources are able to successfully be transported between two OCL servers using export.json files, collection versions are not working in that way. When we try, OCL returns an error `"errors": {  "collection": "Version Head not found."   }`

A user should be able to use a collection version export JSON file from OCL to recreate that collection in another OCL server. Collection versions currently include references, so the MVP of this would be to create the collection (if needed) and then import the references from the collection version JSON. 

Collection version JSON files do NOT include an expansion, but we can ignore that for now and just do an autoexpand.

A next stage for this might be to be responsive to the concepts and mappings in the collection version JSON, but that probably requires more discussion.

1763.title: Concept tracing information - Modification indicators
1763.body: A user needs to be aware if the concept in a repo/collection that they are viewing is "original" (i.e. is the same as the source concept) or "modified" (i.e. its details, mappings, etc. differ from the source concept). In TBv2, this only manifests when the mappings of a concept differ from its original source vs. what is being displayed on the concept from the context of a collection. Complexities from this [OCL Arch Call](https://docs.google.com/document/d/157ChmfmYLGLEg_fO4cE7oMqtGpL4PchUkEhNfUUMxdo/edit#bookmark=id.pbgpc2ccacvm): - Demo concepts: [MSF concept](https://app.openconceptlab.org/#/orgs/MSF/sources/MSF/concepts/1290/) (original from source) vs. [MSF-OCB concept](https://app.openconceptlab.org/#/orgs/MSF-OCB/collections/Trauma/HEAD/expansions/autoexpand-HEAD/concepts/1290/) (modified mappings in collection) (see screenshots below) - Test case - when I add the concept from the MSF-OCB collection, I expect to see the concept and mappings from the MSF-OCB collection. Currently OCL points directly back to the original MSF source’s mappings though. - Complexities: - There’s a balance to strike between the dictionary management/auto-expand perspective vs. the traditional TS/FHIR/non-auto-expand perspective - When viewing the Concepts tab in the context of a collection/valueset, OCL currently displays: - Left: Codes/mappings from their codesystems/sources - If you check concept on the left and “add it to a collection”, the reference points to the source, not the collection - Right: Concept from collection context (cascaded within context), and the link at the top of the details pane is to the source - Header and attributes are populated from source viewpoint - Display name can be set in a reference/valueset – not clear if the TB shows the display name from the ValueSet, or if it only shows the info from the underlying source; but we know that the TB doesn’t handle context or any other types of modifications that a user might eventually be able to make to a concept within a reference - “Associations” section cascades from collection viewpoint Original MSF Concept: "Modified" MSF Concept:

2266.title: Simplified CSV output that combines concepts and mappings
2266.body:
OCL's export formats (Concepts CSV, Mappings CSV, and Export JSON) do not meet user requirements of having a full representation of a repository in a concept-focused way, particularly without limiting the number of resources to 1000.

We are exploring a new format that transforms OCL's export JSON into a concept-anchored CSV (meaning, each row is one concept, and those concepts' mappings are displayed in the same row).

A Python notebook to illustrate this work is here: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1TH8j-95F1D19dgFpbMwhD1_A2eYRepGU?usp=sharing


Potential goals:

> In an ideal world, a new csv format would solve these problems simultaneously:
>  * Export format from OCL
>  * Compatible as an input dataset for OCL Mapper
>  * Compatible with OCL's bulk import format
>  * Very human-readable (Unlike the JSON format)
>  * Comprehensive??? JSON represents 100% of concept metadata -- should this?
>  * Enable representation of collection references?

nodes.body:
Here is a comma-separated syntax for mappings that is backwards compatible with the format used in the concept prevalence study, extensible to more metadata, parseable with a single regular expression, and still human readable. Position of map-type (optional) implies directionality. Looking for feedback.

```
Generic syntax:
[\[<direct-map-type>\] ]<source-reference>:<concept-code>[ "<name>[ \[<locale>\]]"][\ [<inverse-map-type>\]]

Examples:
SNOMED-CT:1292929349
CIEL:1234 "name"
CIEL:1234 "part 1, part 2 [optional-locale]"
ICD10:A01.1 name-with-no-quote
[NARROWER-THAN] ICD-10:AO1.1 "name"
[NARROWER-THAN] ICD-10:AO1.1 "name [en]"

Real example for CIEL 146641:
[NARROWER-THAN] ICD-10:AO1.1, [SAME-AS] ICD-11-WHO:NE02&XA99N3, [SAME-AS] IMO-ProblemIT:22793, [SAME-AS] CIEL:146641 "Burn of uterus [en]", [SAME-AS] SNOMED-CT:22388002, [SAME-AS] ICD-10-WHO:T28.3
```

Notes:
* Location of the map-type implies directionality of the mapping, so this would be valid for inverse maps: <source-reference>:<concept-code>[ "<name>[ \[<locale>\]]"][ \[<inverse-map-type>\]]
* This format would support multiple source reference approaches:
    * Relative URL: /orgs/CIEL/sources/CIEL/
    * Canonical URL:  http://loinc.org/
    * Short-codes: ICD-10-WHO -- use of short-codes would generally require a source mapping table
cc @bmamlin

Questions from @bmamlin:
- How are special characters handled/escaped in names (brackets, commas, quotes)?
- Are single quotes valid or only double quotes?
- Assuming order of mappings doesn’t matter (is not defined).

2281.title: Proposal to support download of a concept's mappings as a valueset
2281.body:
***THIS TICKET IS NOT READY FOR DEVELOPMENT YET***

Thanks to @delcroip for suggesting this feature

**Use Case:**
CIEL:116128 has many mappings: https://app.openconceptlab.org/#/orgs/CIEL/sources/CIEL/concepts/116128/. I want an endpoint to download those mappings (optionally with filtering, e.g. only SAME-AS) as a ValueSet that I can easily refer back to.

**MVP Proposal**
- Implement a new OCL API endpoint with optional filtering (e.g. map-types, repo/source), e.g.: https://api.openconceptlab.org/orgs/CIEL/sources/CIEL/concepts/116128/$getMappingsAsValueSet?mapTypes=SAME-AS,NARROWER-THAN,BROADER-THAN. This is a custom OCL API format -- we expect long-term that we would want a compliant FHIR method to request this, likely via an $expand request with a novel compose statement.
- Return a minimalist FHIR-compliant ValueSet response of a concept’s mappings:
```
{
  "name": "Mappings for CIEL:116128",
  "url": "https://api.openconceptlab.org/orgs/CIEL/sources/CIEL/concepts/116128/$getMappingsAsValueSet?mapTypes=SAME-AS,NARROWER-THAN,BROADER-THAN",
  expansion: {
    contains: []
  }
}
```

nodes.body:
That should work well, but I'm unsure about including parameters in the canonical URL, as FHIR generally discourages them.
I'd prefer a clean pattern like:

https://api.openconceptlab.org/orgs/CIEL/sources/CIEL/concepts/116128/ValueSetMaps/SAME-AS

(or with multiple types: /ValueSetMaps/SAME-AS,NARROWER-THAN,BROADER-THAN)

I explored alternatives like fake filter properties, but they aren't as solid as your approach.

  - Document differences in OCL capabilities and intended/expected FHIR spec -- e.g. what can OCL automatically do even without an explicit property/filter definition (like full text search), what can OCL not do (or do differently) than FHIR expects (e.g. `is_a`)

Note: this requires that a property can be bound to a valueset and validated, which means likely requires more than is available in a property definition alone (e.g. structure definition, profile, etc.)

2159.title: Source properties and filters
2159.body:
## Overview
Source properties and filters is a core capability that OCL is missing that is required for a fully functional FHIR service, and would also provide a lot of value even without a HAPI FHIR Plain Server integration.

Note that even though the requirement is coming from the FHIR spec, we are implementing this as a core OCL capability that will be available in the OCL API for `source` repositories.

## Specification
From the perspective of FHIR, properties and filters are defined in the CodeSystem specification: https://build.fhir.org/codesystem.html

Required ValueSet bindings:
* **property-type**
  * R4B (https://hl7.org/fhir/R4B/valueset-concept-property-type.html): code, Coding, string, integer, boolean, dateTime, decimal
   * R6 (https://build.fhir.org/valueset-concept-property-type.html): same as above
* **filter-operator**
  * R4B (https://hl7.org/fhir/R4B/valueset-filter-operator.html): =, is-a, descendent-of, is-not-a, regex, in, not-in, generalizes, exists
  * R6 (https://build.fhir.org/valueset-filter-operator.html): all of the above, plus… child-of, descendent-leaf

## OCL current state
- **Extras**: OCL implements a source.concept.extras attribute, which is a simple dictionary of key-value pairs.
- **Schema-less**: Extra values are “schema-less”. They may be of any datatype, and it is up to the user/client to validate/enforce datatypes and to determine which are optional or required.
- **Filtering**: OCL supports filtering by any extra attribute if the user knows in advance the extra attribute key that they want to filter – Currently, there is no way to get information about attributes that are in use, which can be used for filtering, and the datatypes of those filters, etc. without traversing the entire CodeSystem/source.
- **Filter Operations**: Not all of the filter operations defined in FHIR (and used commonly by FHIR CodeSystems) are exposed by OCL (Note: OCL uses ElasticSearch, which means that all FHIR filter capabilities are available under the hood – we just need to expose them)

## Sequencing:
- **MVP: Read and save source properties and filters** (but not yet enforce as schema)
  - Implement full representation/CRUD of `source.properties` and `source.filters` attributes in the OCL API and expose CRUD in the TermBrowser
  - CRUD in the native OCL FHIR CodeSystem serializers (note, that this is not at the field level, only at the CodeSystem level)
  - View properties and filters in TBv2 and TBv3 when viewing repositories (as OCL sources) --- not implementing CRUD in TB yet
- **MVP Round 2: Expose in TB search**
  - Migrate class and datatype to become properties (e.g. could copy for now so that there are no breaking changes)
  - Expose source properties and filters for repo-level search in the TermBrowser search interface (including, shifting class/datatype to become properties in the TermBrowser)
  - Implement API ability for a CodeSystem/Source admin to define which properties should be included in the summary of a concept and brought to the top when requesting the list of properties (e.g. CIEL: class and datatype; LOINC: the 6 LOINC parts... Component, Property, Time, System, Scale, Method -- plus Units)
- **MVP Round 3: Enforce properties as schema**
  - Enforce defined properties as a schema for extra attributes in the API
    - Option: Admin controls whether other extra attributes can still be defined
  - Use source properties as a schema to assist users when managing concepts in the TermBrowser
  - Document differences in OCL capabilities and intended/expected FHIR spec -- e.g. what can OCL automatically do even without an explicit property/filter definition (like full text search), what can OCL not do (or do differently) than FHIR expects (e.g. `is_a`)
- **MVP Round 4**
  - Generate/Retrieve a Profile for a source that defines the schema (based on the properties)
  - Support for FHIR’s predefined Concept Properties CodeSystem to help with consistency across codesystems: https://build.fhir.org/codesystem.html#defined-props 
  - Method to store property/filter definitions (e.g. http://loinc.org/property/COMPONENT)

nodes.body: @paynejd do we have example request for filter like "is-a"?

@snyaggarwal the `is_a` filter is a subsumption query. OCL does not currently have this capability, though `$cascade` is the basis for supporting it. Let's leave `is_a` for round 2. We can pull together examples and documentation in the meantime.

##### Notes 
1. I feel it's better to use new fields like `properties` and `filters`. ES provides better search support on a fixed schema


### MVP Round 1 (24 points)
1. `source.properties` -- `ArrayField` with schema
```json
{"code": "string", "uri": "<string-uri>", "description": "string", "type": "string"}
```
2. `source.filters` -- `ArrayField` with schema
```json
{"code": "string", "operator": "<string-uri>", "description": "string", "value": "string"}
```
3. Just like `/extras/` OCL CRUD APIs on `/properties/` and `/filters/`
```text
GET /orgs/MyOrg/sources/MySource/properties/
GET /orgs/MyOrg/sources/MySource/properties/<code>/

```

### MVP Round 2 (32 - 40 points)
1. ES search query builder for each type of operator
2. Exposing the DSL on TB/Swagger


### MVP round 3 (24 points)
1. Property schema validation on `concept.extras` as a separate/new schema validator (similar to OpenMRSSchemaValidator)
2. TB forms validations based on new schema validator


### MVP round 4 (~40 points) -- Need to read bit more here.

Additional notes: 
- Source property and filter definitions would be stored here: `source.properties` and `source.filters`
- Actual concept property data (in round 2) would be stored in `concept.extras`, 

Still needed for MVP round 1:
- Expose CRUD for property and filter definitions in the native OCL FHIR CodeSystem serializers
See a full LOINC codesystem property and filter definition here: https://loinc.org/fhir/

Change display of properties and filters in TBv2 and TBv3 to a tabular format, e.g.:

1666.title: Introduce "Synonym" name type and remove "None" name type
1666.body:
Currently, OCL [name types](https://app.openconceptlab.org/#/orgs/OCL/sources/NameTypes/) include `None` and do not have an entry for `Synonym`.

**Problem with `None`**: Since name type is optional, the difference between `None` and `null` is not defined and, as long as `None` exists, will cause headaches & confusion.

**`Synonym` as default**: an alternate name for a concept implies the very definition of [synonym](https://www.dictionary.com/browse/synonym), so it's natural to assume any name where name type is not specified is a synonym.

1. Purge the [None name type](https://app.openconceptlab.org/#/orgs/OCL/sources/NameTypes/concepts/None/). Any existing data using the `None` name type can be converted to `null`.

2. Add a `Synonym` name type and start treating `null` & `Synonym` the same (i.e., assuming a name of unknown type is an alternate name – a.k.a. synonym).

3. Optionally, once the codebase is treating `null` & `Synonym` the same, then we could elect to make name type required (converting all existing `null` entries to `Synonym`).

nodes.body:
Areas in TermBrowser where this might impact (i.e. where name types come into play):

- Importing/validating content: Imported content may not match what is in OCL
- Forms: Create/Edit Concept
- Screens:
    - Names & Synonyms in Concept Details section
    - Summary tab: Name Types
    - Facets (Global search and repo browsing)
- Checksums
- OpenMRS Validation Schema?
- API Search e.g. `?q=&isTable=true&isList=false&page=1&type=concepts&facets={"nameTypes":{"":true}}` vs. `?q=&isTable=true&isList=false&page=1&type=concepts&facets={"nameTypes":{"None":true}}` 

And an overall question - are we also getting rid of the "None" type for Descriptions too? Or are we only focused on Name Types here?

During an OCL Squad call today, @jamlung-ri was sharing an OCL screen and it was a good example of why this issue needs to be addressed: Notice that some synonyms have name type `None` and another synonym has a blank name type.

790.title: Implement access control for exports
790.body:
Right now, a source or collection is either public or private, and this access control applies to all aspects of that repository. From the perspective of a standards development organization, reference access (searching/browsing) is treated separately from distribution.

How could we allow admins to control reference access to a source/collection separately from distribution access?

For now, we can keep these definitions simple:
* Reference - search, browse, viewing details of resources, basic API access (though we should probably put a ceiling to the `limit` parameter)
* Distribution - users may download full exports

Open questions:
* How do we (and should we) limit mappings and references

@bmamlin FYI

638.title: Allow source/collection admins to control visibility of repo versions separate from tags
638.body:
Right now, the visibility of a source/collection version is tied somewhat to its `released` and `retired` flags. This is especially true when using the `latest` keyword (which evaluates to "latest released version") or when evaluating a version-less reference, like `/orgs/CIEL/sources/CIEL/concepts/1234/`. We want administrators to have complete control over how OCL resolves these pointers, which means letting them set repo version visibility / behavior separately from flags.

In addition, we want to support the rest of the "flags" supported by the FHIR `status` field for CodeSystem, ValueSet, ConceptMap (draft | active | retired | unknown). Here's the publication status ValueSet: https://www.hl7.org/fhir/valueset-publication-status.html

2294.title: $match API performance optimization
2294.body:
Currently running $match with reranker that returns 30 candidates takes around avg 15-20 seconds in production, the breakup of one the request is:

- ES Search built in 0.39279985427856445 seconds
- Cross encoder time: 7.125919818878174 seconds
- ES hits to Objects in 3.99991991999999 seconds
- Serialization in 2.370438814163208 seconds
- Sorting in 0.0003390312194824219 seconds

Encoder taking ~50% of time — we have few options to go with -

- We do smaller batches like 10-15 candidates
- We use GPUs (right now its on CPU only)
- We use some other light encoder
